data.title: Southwest Airlines Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript
data.description:
Southwest Airlines (NYSE:LUV) released first-quarter financial results and hosted an earnings call on Thursday. Read the complete transcript below.

Benzinga APIs provide real-time access to earnings call transcripts and financial data. Visit https://www.benzinga.com/apis/ to learn more.

The full earnings call is available at https://event.choruscall.com/mediaframe/webcast.html?webcastid=JzFOasnS

Summary

Southwest Airlines reported a significant year-over-year improvement with an EPS of $0.45, reflecting strong customer demand and margin expansion despite higher fuel costs.

First quarter operating margin improved by 8.1 points year-over-year to 4.6%, with operating cash flow increasing by 65% to $1.4 billion.

The company's strategic initiatives, including new product offerings and network adjustments, are contributing to record passenger revenue and operating revenue.

Future outlook remains cautious due to economic and geopolitical uncertainties, with a second-quarter EPS guidance of $0.35 to $0.65, influenced by fuel price volatility.

Southwest Airlines continues to focus on cost discipline and operational efficiency, with plans to optimize capacity and further enhance its product offerings, including partnerships with Starlink.

Full Transcript

Nick (Moderator)

Danielle Collins (Managing Director of Investments Relations)

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

Andrew Waterson (Chief Operating Officer)

Tom Doxy (Chief Financial Officer)

Danielle Collins (Managing Director of Investments Relations)

Thank you, Tom. This concludes our prepared remarks. We will now open the line for analyst questions to help us manage time efficiently. We ask that you please ask one or two questions back to back at the onset.

OPERATOR

Thank you, Danielle. Again, to ask a question, press star. Then 1. To withdraw your interest, press star. And then 2. If you are on a speakerphone today, please pick up your handset before pressing any keys. And the first question will come from Mike Lindenberg with Deutsche Bank. Please go ahead.

Mike Lindenberg (Equity Analyst)

Andrew Waterson (Chief Operating Officer)

It's Andrew. I'll start with the first one. I'm not going to break down it kind of fair product by fair product, but I will say that, you know, obviously we had an 11.6% yield increase year over year and at least half of that came from people voluntarily decided to pay more by buying up. So we have kind of secular yield trends going on and then we have people voluntarily buying up which creates the extra yield boost. And so net net.

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Jamie Baker with JP Morgan. Please go ahead.

Jamie Baker (Equity Analyst)

Tom Doxy (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Connor Cunningham with Melius Research. Please go ahead.

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

Tom Doxy (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Kathryn o' Brien with Goldman Sachs. Please go ahead.

Kathryn o' Brien

Andrew Waterson (Chief Operating Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Ravi Shanka with Morgan Stanley. Please go ahead.

Ravi Shanka (Equity Analyst)

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Scott Group with Wolff Research. Please go ahead.

Scott Group

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Dwayne Fenningworth with Evercore isi. Please go ahead.

Dwayne Fenningworth (Equity Analyst)

Tom Doxy (Chief Financial Officer)

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Atul Mahaswari with ubs. Please go ahead.

Atul Mahaswari (Equity Analyst)

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

Tom Doxy (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Sabi Sith with Raymond James. Please go ahead.

Sabi Sith (Equity Analyst)

Tom Doxy (Chief Financial Officer)

Yeah, thanks, Xavi. We had five aircraft sales that we did. There were 337, 737 to seven hundreds. There were two 737, eight hundreds that we sold. So those five aircraft and about a 30 or 40 million dollars book impact there. So not super material to the cost numbers that you saw. So everything you're seeing in the cost numbers is around the structural changes that we're making to the business.

OPERATOR

The next question will come from John Godden with the Citigroup. Please go ahead.

John Godden (Equity Analyst)

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Tom Fitzgerald with TD Cowan. Please go ahead.

Tom Fitzgerald (Equity Analyst)

Andrew Waterson (Chief Operating Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Brandon Oglensky with Barclays. Please go ahead.

Brandon Oglensky (Equity Analyst)

Andrew Waterson (Chief Operating Officer)

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Sheila Kiello with Jeffries. Please go ahead.

Sheila Kiello (Equity Analyst)

Good morning guys and thank you. Maybe just related to all the fuel comments and capacity comments, Bob, I could see why you're frustrated at the same time too, you know, I guess at what fuel price do you make further changes to capacity? And as a follow up to that, you know, how do we think about when fuel prices and how fuel prices impact your aircraft sales or deliveries and how you think about changing them for how long they stay at these elevated levels?

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

Tom Doxy (Chief Financial Officer)

And then the follow on question on aircraft having such a large fleet of, you know, mostly unencumbered owned airplanes gives us tons of flexibility. So that'll really just be an output of how and where we're looking to grow and to what levels and that flexibility is there to retire or retain, to adjust to whatever the environment might be.

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Dan McKenzie with Seaport Global. Please go ahead.

Dan McKenzie (Equity Analyst)

Tom Doxy (Chief Financial Officer)

Andrew Waterson (Chief Operating Officer)

The next question will come from Chris Weatherby with Wells Fargo. Please go ahead.

OPERATOR

Yeah, hey, thanks.

Chris Weatherby

OPERATOR

The next question will come from David Vernon with Bernstein. Please go ahead.

David Vernon (Equity Analyst)

Hey, good morning and thanks for taking the question. So I guess I should say. Yeah, so if you look at the rapid rewards information that's in the earnings release, they're talking about enrollments up 37%.

Andrew Waterson (Chief Operating Officer)

OPERATOR

The next question will come from Chris Stephanopoulos with sig. Please go ahead.

Chris Stephanopoulos (Equity Analyst)

Andrew Waterson (Chief Operating Officer)

Thank you.

Bob Jordan (President and Chief Executive Officer)

This is Andrew. We are seeing extraordinarily strong fares and strong demand across the entire network, across all customer segments, across different travel types. The only place seen weakness are the Mexican beach resorts and Hawaii because of weather and political activities. And even those have seen a sequential improvement in the last couple weeks. So it is. When we say broad based, we very much mean broad based.

OPERATOR

Michael Goldie (Equity Analyst)

Thank you. And the next question will come from Michael Goldie with BMO Capital Markets, please go ahead. Morning and thank you for squeezing me in. Going back to costs for maintenance expense, is the performance that we're seeing driven by delivery of new aircraft and then divesting of older equipment, or is anything else changing that's driving that maintenance performance? And then just a follow up on headcount.

Tom Doxy (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

That wraps up today's call. We appreciate everyone for joining us.

data.title: Eagle Bancorp Reports Q1 2026 Results: Full Earnings Call Transcript
data.description:
Eagle Bancorp (NASDAQ:EGBN) held its first-quarter earnings conference call on Thursday. Below is the complete transcript from the call.

Benzinga APIs provide real-time access to earnings call transcripts and financial data. Visit https://www.benzinga.com/apis/ to learn more.

Access the full call at https://edge.media-server.com/mmc/p/cn74vqnt/

Summary

Eagle Bancorp reported a return to profitability with net income of $14.7 million, or $0.48 per diluted share, reversing the previous quarter's loss. Net interest margin expanded and core deposits grew by $240 million year over year.

The company continues its strategic repositioning by reducing reliance on higher-cost broker deposits and resolving legacy exposures, which has led to elevated charge-offs.

Asset quality showed improvement with a decline in criticized and classified assets, although non-performing loans increased. The allowance for credit losses is $147.2 million, or 2.12% of total loans.

Eagle Bancorp maintained its guidance for 2026, expecting full-year NIM in the 2.6% to 2.8% range, non-interest income growth of 15% to 25%, and flat to down non-interest expenses.

Management highlighted the success in diversifying the balance sheet, reducing concentration risks, and maintaining strong capital positions with tangible common equity of 11.51%.

Full Transcript

OPERATOR

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

Susan Real (President and CEO)

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

Susan Real (President and CEO)

OPERATOR

Thank you so much. And as a reminder to ask a question, press star 11 on your telephone and wait for your name to be announced. To remove yourself, press Star one one again. One moment. While we compile the Q and A roster, Our first question comes from the line of Justin Crowley with Piper Sandler. Please proceed.

Justin Crowley (Equity Analyst)

Hey, good morning everyone. Just wanted to start off on the level of criticized here. You know, obviously good to see that continue to fall with some help from the loan sales. But just curious if you can speak a little more to the new inflows and to criticize. Eric and I hit on that a little bit. But you know, is that a, you know, a pace that you'd expect slows from here or how are you thinking about the trajectory as you move through the year?

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Hey Justin, this is Ryan. I think the, you know, forecasting, forecasting what that is is tough to do. Our portfolio management practices touch on these loans each and every quarter. We shouldn't see many surprises in that process because we touch it so frequently, but it is expected to continue some migration in there.

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

Ultimately though, just to build off of that, Justin, our goal and and commitment in my prepared commentary is that criticized classified will continue to come down on an absolute level as well as relative to loans and tier one capital. So we're going to make some, we're expected based on what we see today and what we believe and we're going to make some meaningful progress by year end. Right.

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

And Justin, to build on that, it's important for us to note that the regulatory definition of criticized and classified loans does not Require loss content. Right. The potential weakness or well defined weakness that would define those roles don't necessarily have loss content in them. And that's part of the story we've been telling for several quarters. As you've seen the composition of that list fall away from office.

Justin Crowley (Equity Analyst)

Okay, got it. And then so I guess you know, in terms of further loan sales, you know, with what's remaining in held for sale and you know, I guess anything that could get added from here. Are we at a point where future disposals are largely coming outside of the office portfolio? You know, how would you set expectations there in terms of, you know, what you're looking to ring fed?

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

I think we're looking at each and every case on a one off basis. We're evaluating it. Management comes to a decision as to what the best path forward is and we use the tools at our discretion that are there. The anticipation is that tactic will continue to be used as we determine it is the best path to reach the best possible outcome and maximize shareholder value.

Justin Crowley (Equity Analyst)

Okay, that's fair. And then I guess just one last one on the office reserve which you took down in the quarter, can you talk through just a little more on some of the factors that get you comfortable in that decision? You know, I guess in part considering, you know, the increase in non accruals, you know, with a lot of that

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

Justin Crowley (Equity Analyst)

Okay, great. I will leave it there. Thanks for taking the question.

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Thanks Justin.

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from David Chiaverini with Jefferies. Please proceed.

David Chiaverini (Equity Analyst)

Hi, thanks for taking the questions. Wanted to follow up on this credit quality discussion. Good to see that criticized. Classified down and it sounds like you're expecting a continued decline through this year. Does this commentary also apply to the non accrual loans that we saw increase in the quarter?

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

David Chiaverini (Equity Analyst)

Great, thanks for that. And on the held for sale portfolio and the sales that you have under contract, are you selling them? It looks like there was on slide 18 a valuation adjustment of about $3 million. Are you selling loans in line with what you originally thought? And I guess to ask another way, curious, what percent of par on average are you selling loans?

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

David Chiaverini (Equity Analyst)

Right, got it. But and then, you know, as a percentage of carrying value and the reserves, netting the reserves against that part, you know, significantly higher is my assumption. Would you say that's fair?

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Well, and that's what Eric's comments address. And that the reduction to the carrying value netted us for that portfolio. Right about at new par, if you will.

David Chiaverini (Equity Analyst)

Very helpful. Thank you.

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from the line of Catherine Miller with kbw. Please proceed. Thanks.

Catherine Miller (Equity Analyst)

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

Catherine Miller (Equity Analyst)

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Catherine Miller (Equity Analyst)

Great, thank you.

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our next question comes from the line of Steve Moth with Raymond James. Please proceed.

Steve Moth (Equity Analyst)

Good morning. Maybe just following up on your comments there Ryan. With regard to the couple of new inflows. You have the hotel motel in Arlington and the Prince George's county apartment building as well. Those matured here in the last couple weeks. Just kind of curious, you know, did you give them extensions or kind of how to, you know, what's kind of the expectation there in terms of where you're going with those credits?

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Yeah, we had hoped obviously before the maturity date to have a longer term plan in place. We didn't arrive at that. So we did put short term extensions in place in both of those situations which have already been booked. It's obviously the Data says of 331. So the current maturity is actually out into the future a bit and we continue to work with each of those clients for a longer term solution.

Steve Moth (Equity Analyst)

Okay, got you. In terms of kind of like thinking about the overall Washington D.C. market I know there's different sub markets have issues and stuff but just kind of curious maybe just stepping back, what's your overall sense of activity especially for the multifamily space in terms of renting out properties and where things are going.

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Steve Moth (Equity Analyst)

Okay. And then maybe similar question on the office side here. You know, my sense has been there's just been better lease up activity with, with regards to office in, in some of the markets. And just kind of curious, you know, what's. What are you seeing for office activity here? You know, are the green shoots still there? Have they moderated from maybe a few months ago? Thoughts on that?

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Steve Moth (Equity Analyst)

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

I think it's important just building off of that, it's important to reemphasize though the commitment that the team has to reducing the overall criticizing classified on an absolute basis by year end. And we're going to continue to show progress here in future quarters as well.

Steve Moth (Equity Analyst)

All right, great. I appreciate all the call here. Thank you very much guys.

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Thanks, Steve.

OPERATOR

Thank you. Our last question comes from the line of Christopher Marinek with Breem Capital llc. Please proceed.

Christopher Marinek

Good morning. I want to I wanted to ask about the sort of granularity point that Ryan was just making. Is that going to work in your favor in terms of inflows possibly being less because of the smaller sized loans as you continue to work through the book?

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Short answer is yes.

Christopher Marinek

Ryan Reel (Chief Lending Officer for Commercial Real Estate)

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

Christopher Marinek

Great, thank you Eric, appreciate that. And just last question for me. I was on the FDIC expense. Is that going to be lumpy in terms of how it comes off in future quarters? Was this quarter any indication of kind of where it could go in the near term?

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

Christopher Marinek

Okay. And half is still using this March pace that we just saw.

Eric Newell (Chief Financial Officer)

I would. You know what, Chris? I would take Our full year 25 Number and use that as the basis.

Christopher Marinek

Great. Thanks for clarifying. Great. Thanks for clarifying that. And thank you all for all the information this morning.

OPERATOR

Thanks, Chris.

Susan Real (President and CEO)

Thank you. Ladies and gentlemen, this will conclude the Q and A session. I will pass it back to the president and CEO Susan Real, for closing remarks.

OPERATOR

I want to thank all of you for your participation and your questions today, and we look forward to talking to you again next quarter. Have a great day.

data.title: Bitcoin Taps $78,000, But The Road To $80,000 Could Be Even Harder
data.description:
Bitcoin (CRYPTO: BTC) has broken above the True Market Mean at $78,100 for the first time since mid-January, but Glassnode data shows profit-taking spiked to levels that marked every local top this year.

The $80,000 Resistance Wall

The Short-Term Holder Cost Basis at $80,100 represents the average acquisition price of investors who purchased within the last 155 days. 

A recovery toward $80,000 would push more than 54% of recent buyers into profit, historically the threshold where distribution pressure has exhausted bear market rallies.

“This is the second instance of this structure forming,” Glassnode analysts wrote. “Repeated encounters with this threshold reinforce its reliability as a local top indicator,” they added.

Short-Term Holder Realized Profit spiked to $4.4 million per hour. Every prior spike above $1.5 million per hour has coincided with a local top formation. The current reading is nearly three times that warning threshold.

Futures Driving The Rally, Not Spot

Julio Moreno, an on-chain analyst, warned that the recent Bitcoin price increase is “completely driven by demand in the perpetual futures market” while spot demand is still contracting. 

“The same happened in January, when Bitcoin peaked at $98K,” Moreno said.

Moreover “there are risks of a correction if traders start taking profits while spot demand continues to contract,” he added.

ETF Flows Turn Positive Again

U.S. Spot ETF flows have begun to recover, with the 7-day moving average shifting back into positive territory after a prolonged period of sustained outflows. 

This marks a notable change in institutional demand following heavy distribution throughout late January and February.

The recent inflow clusters suggest renewed allocation from traditional investors, coinciding with Bitcoin’s rebound from the approximately $65,000 lows into the mid-$70,000s.

Negative Funding Rates Build Short Squeeze Potential

Perpetual funding rates have shifted decisively lower, with sustained prints in negative territory across most major exchanges. 

This reflects a market increasingly skewed toward short positioning.

“Crowded short positioning can act as fuel for upside moves, particularly if spot demand re-emerges,” Glassnode analysts wrote.

The Gamma Setup

A large concentration of negative gamma sits below the current price, particularly in the $75,000 region. 

With Bitcoin trading around $79,000, the immediate upside shifts into a long gamma area where hedging flows tend to dampen moves, creating mechanical resistance.

“A move back toward the mid-$75,000 region would bring price into the short gamma zone, where dealer hedging can accelerate downside price action,” Glassnode noted.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Mullen Group Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript
data.description:
Mullen Group (TSX:MTL) reported first-quarter financial results on Thursday. The transcript from the company's first-quarter earnings call has been provided below.

This content is powered by Benzinga APIs. For comprehensive financial data and transcripts, visit https://www.benzinga.com/apis/.

Access the full call at https://event.choruscall.com/mediaframe/webcast.html?webcastid=wQX1kXWy

Summary

Mullen Group reported record revenues and solid profitability despite minimal economic growth, emphasizing a focus on cost management and margin over market share.

The company's LNW segment showed strong organic growth, driven by a robust March, although the LTL segment remains stagnant due to factors like demarketing some customers and inclement weather.

Future growth is anticipated with potential nation-building projects and strategic acquisitions, particularly in the SNI segment, though the timing and certainty of these projects remain unclear.

Management highlighted an increased focus on AI integration across operations, particularly in U.S. business units, to enhance market share and operational efficiency.

The company is cautious about the impact of rising fuel prices on the economy and consumer behavior, which could affect future earnings, despite a generally optimistic outlook for sustained growth.

Full Transcript

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

OPERATOR

Thank you. We will now begin the question and answer session. To join the question queue, you may press star then 1. On your telephone keypad, you will hear a tone acknowledging your request. If you are using a speakerphone, please pick up your handset before pressing any keys. To withdraw your question, please press Star then two. The first question comes from Kevin Chang from cibc. Please go ahead.

Kevin Chang (Equity Analyst at CIBC)

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Kevin Chang (Equity Analyst at CIBC)

Maybe just on the LTL side, maybe if I could follow up on your answer there, Just the momentum you saw in March in LTL and W, as I carried on through first few weeks here in April and just on LTL, I guess some of that organic growth rate that you saw, or negative organic growth rate you saw, it seems like you were demarketing some businesses. Is there a way to quantify maybe how much that weighed on the first quarter growth rate?

Carson Erlacher (Senior Financial Officer)

Kevin Chang (Equity Analyst at CIBC)

No, it seems like it was winter everywhere, by my observation. And just on lnw, just the April trends, has that momentum carried forward from what you saw in March there?

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Kevin Chang (Equity Analyst at CIBC)

Richard Maloney (President and Senior Operating Officer)

Kevin Chang (Equity Analyst at CIBC)

Hey, how are you doing?

Richard Maloney (President and Senior Operating Officer)

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Kevin Chang (Equity Analyst at CIBC)

That's very helpful. Congrats on a solid start to the year here, despite all the weather issues. Thank you guys. Thanks very much.

OPERATOR

The next question comes from Konark Gupta from Scotiabank. Please go ahead.

Konark Gupta (Equity Analyst at Scotiabank)

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Konark Gupta (Equity Analyst at Scotiabank)

Okay, that makes sense. The other side of the coin I guess is if the US administration keeps pushing out these drivers from the pool and I think the numbers are quite staggering. If you look in some of the studies would you expect or would you see some sort of wage inflation or maybe not so much given your drivers are still sticky. I mean you don't have any retention issues.

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Konark Gupta (Equity Analyst at Scotiabank)

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Richard Maloney (President and Senior Operating Officer)

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Konark Gupta (Equity Analyst at Scotiabank)

I appreciate the time. Thank you.

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Thank you. Thanks a lot.

OPERATOR

The next question comes from Cameron Dorksen from National Bank. Please go ahead.

Cameron Dorksen (Equity Analyst at National Bank)

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Richard Maloney (President and Senior Operating Officer)

Cameron Dorksen (Equity Analyst at National Bank)

Carson Erlacher (Senior Financial Officer)

Carson, I'll defer that to you and I'll make a final comment on that. Sure. No change to the guide right now. Cameron, as we're coming out of the first quarter, I would say by segment, everything is largely in line with what we articulated back at the beginning of the year. So I would say no material changes to the guide that we kicked out in January. If March holds and we continue on that trend, I don't see any.

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Yeah, I mean, if the March trend was, was sustained, we'd be above. We would. So, but, and I think the other thing is, Cam, is that we didn't plan any nation building projects in our, in our plan, in our budget for this year. So if those start to go, that's on top of, you know, what, what we said we would do.

Carson Erlacher (Senior Financial Officer)

And that doesn't include any acquisition. Acquisition and no additional acquisition. So, you know, we've got a couple, a couple things that we're, you know, we're, we're going to, you know, maybe go ahead and beat that. But you know, that's not built into the, in the, in the plan that we put forward.

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Said look, this is what we think our existing business will do and so far we're on track for it.

Cameron Dorksen (Equity Analyst at National Bank)

No, that's, that's great to hear. I'll pass the line.

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Thanks very much.

Cameron Dorksen (Equity Analyst at National Bank)

Thank you, Ken. Thank you.

OPERATOR

The next question comes from Benoy Poirier from Desjardins. Please go ahead.

Benoy Poirier (Equity Analyst at Desjardins)

Good morning, everyone and congrats, Richard, for your new role.

Richard Maloney (President and Senior Operating Officer)

Thank you.

Benoy Poirier (Equity Analyst at Desjardins)

Yeah. Talking about the opportunity in Alaska, the LNG project, could you maybe provide more color about the potential size of this opportunity and is it your understanding that there's a limited number of companies that could handle such a project?

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Benoy Poirier (Equity Analyst at Desjardins)

That's really great color, Maria. And regarding the SNI segment, with the increase in oil prices, have you already started to see a pickup in drilling and other activity from customers, and are our customers beginning to try and lock up capacity for the months ahead?

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Richard Maloney (President and Senior Operating Officer)

One data point, Benoit, is that the active rig count is still below what it was last year.

Benoy Poirier (Equity Analyst at Desjardins)

Yeah. No. Okay. Okay, great caller. And on the M and A, you made some comments before, but any thoughts on on the current landscape and what about the deals that are crossing your debts these days? What segments are seeing the most seller activity, and have you seen any change in seller expectation given the encouraging signs we see across the industry?

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Benoy Poirier (Equity Analyst at Desjardins)

Richard Maloney (President and Senior Operating Officer)

Benoy Poirier (Equity Analyst at Desjardins)

the second quarter and maybe last one for me very quickly. You mentioned Marie that the LTL still stuck in neutral. What are kind of the key indicators that you're looking at? We've seen capacity tightening in the tl. So is it kind of the signs that you're looking at expecting that the natural LTL volume will flow back to the LTL market? That could provide some help.

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Benoy Poirier (Equity Analyst at Desjardins)

That's great. Thank you very much. Great call. Thank you very much. Thank you.

OPERATOR

The next question comes from Tim James from TD Securities. Please go ahead.

Tim James (Equity Analyst at TD Securities)

Morning, Tim. Good morning. Thank you very much for your time today. My first question we touched earlier on the demarketing of customers noted in a couple of segments. I'm just wondering if it's unusually significant, the demarketing that's sort of been going on since the start of the new year or is this kind of normal conditions that we would have seen last year or would see on a normal run rate basis.

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Tim James (Equity Analyst at TD Securities)

Okay, so the pace of demarketing then really is slowed down this year. This primarily a 25 issue. Okay, okay, that's helpful for sure. And then I was actually going to ask you, you touched on it, the container world. There was some weakness called out in the quarter. Is that primarily what we're talking about is some of the ocean freight? Like I'm just wondering what.

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Tim James (Equity Analyst at TD Securities)

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Tim James (Equity Analyst at TD Securities)

My last question just around fuel and fuel surcharge revenue. Is it reasonable to assume that when we get into Q2 just because of the timing of the increase in fuel prices, that there's a bit more weight there on earnings or drag because of the time lag between fuel surcharge revenue and the expenses? Would it be a bit more of a headwind in Q2 even relative to Q2 to Q1?

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Tim James (Equity Analyst at TD Securities)

Okay, that's very helpful. Thank you very much.

OPERATOR

Thank you once again. If you have a question, please press Star one. The next question comes from Walter Sprachlin from rbc. Please go ahead.

Walter Sprachlin (Equity Analyst at RBC)

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Walter Sprachlin (Equity Analyst at RBC)

Okay. That's great. Thanks very much for the time. Excellent. Thank you.

OPERATOR

This concludes our question and answer session. I would like to turn the conference back over to Mr. Mullen for closing remarks.

Murray Mullen (Chair and Senior Executive Officer)

Thanks for joining us, folks. It's been a full morning already. Everybody's busy. Thanks for everything. We had a pretty good quarter. We were working hard, hard to make sure that we continue to produce great results. And we look forward to chatting with you at the end of second quarter. So thank you for joining us.

data.title: Lululemon Athletica New CEO Pick Triggers Transition Jitters, Stock Slides
data.description:
The stock's decline comes as the company named Heidi O'Neill as its next leader, effective September 8, 2026, which may have raised concerns among investors about the transition period and its potential impact on the company's direction.

CEO Transition Weighs On Sentiment

Heidi O'Neill, a former executive at Nike Inc. (NYSE:NKE), will take over as CEO, succeeding interim co-CEOs Meghan Frank and André Maestrini.

The board expressed confidence in O'Neill's ability to drive growth and innovation within the company.

Governance Pressure And Strategic Disconnect

In March, founder Chip Wilson publicly pressed the athletic apparel retailer's leadership for greater accountability.

Wilson, one of the company's largest shareholders, argued that Lululemon continues to face a longstanding issue — a disconnect between its creative engine and the board's understanding of brand-building fundamentals.

Earnings Beat Overshadowed By Soft Outlook

Lululemon reported quarterly earnings of $5.01 per share, surpassing the consensus estimate of $4.79, while quarterly revenue reached $3.64 billion, exceeding analyst expectations of $3.59 billion.

However, the company's outlook for fiscal 2026 projected earnings between $12.10 and $12.30 per share, which is below the analyst estimate of $12.67, and revenue expectations of $11.35 billion to $11.5 billion also fell short of the $11.53 billion estimate.

The Wall Street Journal said O'Neill's mandate includes reviving Lululemon's U.S. business, where the brand has ceded some dominance in athletic apparel.

By mid-December, Elliott Investment Management had accumulated a stake exceeding $1 billion and advocated for Jane Nielsen, former CFO and COO at Ralph Lauren, to become CEO, according to the report.

Lululemon has recently highlighted new product launches under creative director Jonathan Cheung as a driver of higher full-price sales.

The company is also redesigning stores—reducing clutter and improving visual merchandising—to streamline the customer experience.

LULU Price Action: Lululemon Athletica shares were down 11.76% at $151.01 at the time of publication on Thursday. The stock is near its 52-week low of $143.96, according to Benzinga Pro data.
